Msyl1.brief: A constant array of string abbreviations used for generating names or identifiers in game content.
Msyl1.details: Msyl1 is a fixed array of String objects, each representing a short abbreviation used in various name and identifier generation routines within the game. These abbreviations are selected to ensure diversity and consistency when creating dynamic names, such as for quest mobs or other in-game entities. The array is initialized with a predefined set of abbreviations and is referenced throughout the codebase, particularly in functions like generate_skillquest_mob, where it contributes to constructing names based on attributes like sex or level. The String class used extends std::string, providing additional manipulation features that facilitate flexible string handling in these routines.
Msyl1.notes: The array contains a mixture of alphabetic abbreviations, some of which are reused for different contexts, indicating a pattern of abbreviation reuse for related entities. Its const qualifier ensures the set remains unchanged at runtime, preserving naming conventions.
Msyl1.rationale: This variable exists to provide a standardized set of abbreviations that support consistent and varied name generation for game entities, enhancing the diversity and readability of dynamically created content.
Msyl1.name: Msyl1

usages.quest_8cc, void generate_skillquest_mob(: Used as a source for part of the generated name when the questmob's sex attribute is 1.

Msyl2.brief: An array of string fragments used for constructing names or identifiers in the game.
Msyl2.details: Msyl2 is a constant array of String objects containing various syllables or name segments, which are used to dynamically generate or assemble names and identifiers for in-game entities. These fragments are initialized with specific string literals representing different phonetic components or name parts. They are employed in functions such as generate_skillquest_mob to create diverse character or mob names, often influenced by attributes like gender or level, thereby adding variety and uniqueness to in-game names. The array's design facilitates flexible, randomized name creation, contributing to a richer game environment.
Msyl2.notes: The array is stored as a constant, ensuring immutability during runtime. It is used primarily for name generation, often in conjunction with other string components, to produce varied and contextually appropriate names.
Msyl2.rationale: Msyl2 exists to provide a set of predefined name segments that support the creation of diverse and contextually relevant names for characters and mobs, enhancing the game's immersion and reducing repetitive naming patterns.
Msyl2.name: Msyl2

usages.quest_8cc, void generate_skillquest_mob(: Used as a middle part of the generated name when the questmob's sex attribute is 1.

Msyl3.brief: A constant array of string fragments used for constructing names or identifiers in the game.
Msyl3.details: Msyl3 is a fixed array of String objects containing various syllable or fragment strings, which serve as components for generating names, titles, or other identifiers within the game environment. These fragments are utilized in name construction functions, such as generate_skillquest_mob, where they contribute to forming the suffix or last part of generated names, especially when certain character attributes like sex are involved. The array provides a standardized set of name components that support consistent naming conventions and thematic diversity across game entities. Its design facilitates efficient name generation and ensures variation while maintaining thematic coherence.
Msyl3.notes: The array is stored as a constant to prevent modification, ensuring consistent name generation patterns. It is primarily used as a source of suffixes or name fragments in name assembly routines.
Msyl3.rationale: Msyl3 exists to provide a predefined set of name fragments that enable dynamic and thematically appropriate name generation for characters and entities, enhancing the game's immersive experience and maintaining naming consistency across different contexts.
Msyl3.name: Msyl3

Fsyl1.details: Fsyl1 is a fixed array of String objects containing a diverse set of abbreviations or syllable-like segments. The array is initialized with a predefined list of string literals and is referenced in various parts of the codebase, such as name creation routines for characters, quests, or mobs. Its design facilitates the construction of meaningful or thematic names by combining these segments, as seen in functions like generate_skillquest_mob, which incorporates these strings when forming quest mob names based on character attributes.
Fsyl1.notes: The array is stored as a constant to prevent modification, ensuring the integrity of the predefined abbreviations. Its usage pattern involves indexing and concatenation to assemble names or identifiers dynamically during runtime.

The array is initialized with a curated set of phonetic or thematic syllables, ensuring diversity and consistency in generated names. MagT_table.brief: A constant table that maps magical profession identifiers to their corresponding gender-specific titles.
MagT_table.details: MagT_table is a fixed, immutable list of MagT_type structures, each representing a distinct magical profession or role with associated gender-specific titles. It serves as a centralized reference for retrieving appropriate titles based on a character's magical specialization and gender. The table is initialized with a comprehensive set of entries covering various magical roles, ensuring consistency and ease of access throughout the codebase. It is primarily utilized in functions responsible for assigning or displaying character titles, such as in generate_skillquest_mob, where it aids in selecting the correct title based on context and character attributes. Its static nature guarantees reliable and uniform title references across different modules and features.
MagT_table.notes: Since MagT_table is declared as a constant, it is stored in read-only memory, which helps prevent accidental modifications and optimizes access performance. Its structure facilitates straightforward lookups and ensures that title data remains consistent throughout the application's runtime.
MagT_table.rationale: This table exists to provide a standardized and easily maintainable mapping between magical profession identifiers and their gender-specific titles. It supports consistent character representation and simplifies the process of title assignment and display, thereby enhancing code clarity and reducing duplication.
MagT_table.name: MagT_table

usages.quest_8cc, void generate_skillquest_mob(: Accessed to select a title and assign act_flags when the random switch case chooses magic-themed titles.

CleT_table.brief: A constant list of character titles representing various religious, spiritual, and role-based designations.
CleT_table.details: The `CleT_table` is a static, constant vector containing `CleT_type` entries, each representing a specific character title or role such as 'cleric', 'shaman', or 'bishop'. Each entry includes gender-specific strings used for display and contextual purposes within the game system. This table is utilized during character creation, role assignment, and in-game displays to assign or retrieve appropriate titles based on character attributes or gameplay events. It provides a centralized, predefined set of titles that support consistent representation across different modules, facilitating easy updates and maintenance of role labels.
CleT_table.notes: The table includes gender-specific variants for certain titles, supporting gender-aware display logic. It is designed for read-only access, ensuring the integrity of predefined role names throughout the game.
CleT_table.rationale: This variable exists to provide a standardized, easily accessible collection of character titles that can be used consistently across the game system, supporting clear role identification and display, especially in contexts involving character creation, role assignment, and quest interactions.
CleT_table.name: CleT_table

usages.quest_8cc, void generate_skillquest_mob(: Accessed to select a title and assign act_flags when the random switch case chooses cleric-themed titles.

ThiT_table.brief: A constant vector containing character titles for various thief and rogue archetypes.
ThiT_table.details: ThiT_table is an immutable list of ThiT_type structures, each representing a specific thief or rogue title with gender-specific identifiers. It serves as a centralized repository for character titles used in character generation, display, and role assignment within the application. The table is initialized with a predefined set of titles, including both male and female variants, ensuring consistency in how character roles are referenced and presented across different modules. Its usage is exemplified in functions like generate_skillquest_mob, where it helps assign appropriate titles based on context.
ThiT_table.notes: The table's constant nature ensures thread safety and prevents accidental modifications, maintaining data integrity across the system.
ThiT_table.rationale: ThiT_table exists to standardize and streamline the assignment of character titles for thief and rogue archetypes, supporting consistent role representation and gender-specific terminology throughout the application.
ThiT_table.name: ThiT_table

usages.quest_8cc, void generate_skillquest_mob(: Accessed to select a title and assign act_flags when the random switch case chooses thief-themed titles.

WarT_table.brief: A constant vector that stores pairs of role identifiers and their corresponding gender-specific titles.
WarT_table.details: The `WarT_table` is a predefined, immutable list of `WarT_type` structures, each representing a character role or class along with its associated gender-specific title. It serves as a centralized repository for role-title mappings used throughout the application, particularly in character creation, display, and quest-related functions. The table is initialized with a fixed set of role and title pairs, ensuring consistency in how character roles are referenced and presented across different modules. Its design supports easy retrieval of appropriate titles based on character attributes, facilitating localization and role-based customization in various game scenarios.
WarT_table.notes: The table includes gender-specific titles, which are used to accurately reflect character gender in role descriptions. It is stored as a constant to prevent modification, ensuring data integrity across the system.
WarT_table.rationale: This variable exists to provide a standardized and easily accessible set of role titles that can be used for character identification and display purposes, supporting consistent role representation and localization throughout the game.
WarT_table.name: WarT_table

usages.quest_8cc, void generate_skillquest_mob(: Accessed to select a title and assign act_flags when the random switch case chooses warrior-themed titles.

chan_table.brief: A constant vector defining the properties and configurations for various communication channels in the game.
chan_table.details: The `chan_table` is a static, immutable collection of `chan_type` structures, each representing a specific communication channel such as gossip, flame, qwest, and others. Each entry includes the channel's name, display prefix, message format, associated bit flags for enabling or disabling the channel, revoke bits for permission management, and color formatting identifiers. This table serves as a central reference for configuring, displaying, and controlling communication channels across the system, supporting features like message formatting, permission enforcement, and visual consistency. It is initialized with a predefined set of channels that facilitate social interaction, role-playing, administrative communication, and system notifications within the game environment.
chan_table.notes: The `chan_table` is designed as a constant, ensuring its definitions remain unchanged at runtime, which supports consistent behavior across the application. Its entries are used extensively in functions like `consumer_func(...)` to retrieve channel-specific configurations for message formatting, permission checks, and UI display. The inclusion of color slot identifiers and revoke flags allows for flexible customization and dynamic permission management, respectively.
chan_table.rationale: This variable exists to provide a centralized, standardized configuration for all communication channels, enabling consistent message formatting, permission enforcement, and visual representation. It simplifies the management of multiple channels by consolidating their properties in one place, supporting flexible control over communication features and enhancing user experience through clear, uniform interfaces.
chan_table.name: chan_table

usages.channels_8cc, void channel(Character *ch: Provides configuration data for the specified channel, including its name, prefix, bit flags, revoke bits, and color formatting, which are used throughout the function.

revoke_table.brief: A constant list of revoke_type entries that define various communication and privilege revocations for characters.
revoke_table.details: The revoke_table is a static, comprehensive collection of revoke_type structures, each representing a specific permission or communication restriction such as channel access, messaging capabilities, or experience point modifications. It serves as a centralized reference for managing revocation options, enabling efficient lookup, display, and toggling of privileges during gameplay or system operations. Each entry includes a unique name identifier, an associated bit flag used for bitwise operations, and a descriptive message explaining the purpose of the revoke. This table supports functions that process revocation commands, ensuring consistent handling of privilege changes across the system, and facilitates user interface elements that list or modify revoke states. Its design allows for scalable management of complex permission scenarios, integrating seamlessly with permission checks and status updates.
revoke_table.notes: The revoke_table is designed for efficient bitwise operations and easy user communication. Its entries correspond directly to specific flags used throughout the codebase, ensuring consistency in permission management. The structure supports dynamic privilege toggling and status reporting, making it a core component for permission control systems.
revoke_table.rationale: This table exists to provide a clear, maintainable, and scalable way to manage a wide range of character privileges and communication restrictions. By centralizing revoke definitions, it simplifies permission checks, enhances code readability, and supports flexible game mechanics or system policies that require dynamic privilege adjustments.
revoke_table.name: revoke_table

usages.wiz__secure_8cc, void do_revoke(Characte: Iterated over to list available revoke options, check if an argument matches an option, and determine which revoke flag to toggle.

cgroup_flags.brief: A constant list of command group flags used for categorizing and managing command permissions and memberships.
cgroup_flags.details: The variable `cgroup_flags` is a constant vector containing `flag_type` structures, each representing a specific command group category within the system. These flags are initialized with descriptive names, associated group constants, and a mutability indicator set to true. They serve as a centralized collection for categorizing commands and entities, enabling consistent permission checks, user interface displays, and command management. The list includes groups such as 'General', 'Quest', 'Building', 'Coder', 'Security', 'Player', 'Mobile', 'Clan', 'Avatar', 'Hero', 'Deputy', and 'Leader'. These flags are iterated over in various functions to determine group membership, display group information, and facilitate command categorization, supporting modular and organized handling of command permissions and roles across the codebase.
cgroup_flags.notes: The list is designed to be immutable (`const`) to ensure consistent reference throughout the system. It is used in conjunction with functions that interpret or display group flags, and its structure supports efficient iteration for permission and role management.
cgroup_flags.rationale: This variable exists to provide a unified, easily accessible collection of command group identifiers, supporting the system's need for organized permission management, role assignment, and command categorization. It simplifies the process of checking group memberships and ensures consistency across different modules that handle command permissions and user roles.
cgroup_flags.name: cgroup_flags

usages.wiz__gen_8cc, void do_wizgroup(Character: Iterated over to match the group name prefix and determine which group flag to add or remove.

wiznet_table.brief: A constant vector defining various WizNet notification categories and their associated flags and descriptions.
wiznet_table.details: The `wiznet_table` is a static, predefined collection of `wiznet_type` structures, each representing a specific category or type of WizNet notification used within the system. It initializes multiple entries with identifiers, corresponding flag constants, and descriptive messages that clarify the purpose of each category, such as login events, death notifications, quest updates, and administrative alerts. This table serves as a centralized reference for managing WizNet notification settings, enabling consistent filtering, display, and broadcasting of messages based on user permissions, message types, and context. It is utilized throughout the codebase in functions that iterate over these entries to generate user options, check active notifications, or process specific WizNet categories for broadcasting or logging purposes.
wiznet_table.notes: The table uses a constant vector with entries that include flag constants and descriptive strings, facilitating easy iteration and lookup. It supports scalable management of notification categories by encapsulating related attributes in a structured manner.
wiznet_table.rationale: This variable exists to provide a comprehensive, organized list of all WizNet notification categories, enabling flexible and consistent handling of in-game and administrative messages. It supports permission-based filtering, user customization, and system monitoring by standardizing how notification types are referenced and described across the system.
wiznet_table.name: wiznet_table

raffects.brief: An array of remort affect definitions that specify various buffs, vulnerabilities, and resistances applied to characters during remort.
raffects.details: The `raffects` array consists of constant structures that define a wide range of remort effects, including positive enhancements, vulnerabilities, and resistances. Each entry includes descriptive text, a short identifier, grouping information to prevent duplicate effects within the same group, application chance, and additional parameters such as added vulnerabilities or resistances represented by specific flags. These effects are utilized during the remort process to modify character attributes, providing strategic gameplay options and diversity. The array is initialized with predefined effects categorized into beneficial, detrimental, and neutral types, with some effects linked to specific damage vulnerabilities or resistances via bit flags. These flags influence damage calculations, resistances, and vulnerabilities during combat, enabling nuanced gameplay mechanics and character customization.
raffects.notes: The `raffects` array uses bit flags to efficiently encode vulnerabilities and resistances, allowing multiple effects to be combined and checked quickly during damage processing. Effects in the vulnerabilities and resistances categories are distinguished by their ID ranges and associated flags, supporting flexible and scalable attribute management.
raffects.rationale: This array exists to standardize and organize the various effects that can be applied during remort, ensuring consistent application of buffs, vulnerabilities, and resistances. It supports gameplay mechanics that depend on character vulnerabilities and resistances, enabling dynamic combat interactions and character development strategies.
raffects.name: raffects

position_table.brief: A constant lookup table that maps character position identifiers to their descriptive names and abbreviations.
position_table.details: The position_table is a statically defined array of position_type objects, each representing a specific stance or state a character can be in, such as sleeping, fighting, or flying. It is used throughout the codebase for position-related lookups, iteration, and display purposes. The table is initialized with predefined position descriptions and their corresponding abbreviations, ensuring consistent representation of character positions across different modules and functions.
position_table.notes: Since position_table is a constant global array, it is optimized for read-only access and does not change during runtime, supporting efficient lookups and display operations.
position_table.rationale: This table provides a centralized and standardized mapping of position identifiers to their descriptive names and abbreviations, facilitating consistent user interface output and internal logic handling of character states.
position_table.name: position_table

sex_table.brief: A constant list of predefined sex categories used for character attributes and lookups.
sex_table.details: The `sex_table` is a static, immutable vector containing `sex_type` objects that represent the various sex or gender categories recognized within the application. It includes entries such as "none", "male", "female", and "either", providing a standardized set of options for character attributes, display functions, and lookup operations. This table is referenced across multiple functions for converting sex identifiers to human-readable strings, performing comparisons, and ensuring consistent categorization. Its fixed initialization guarantees uniformity in how sex information is handled throughout the codebase.
sex_table.notes: The table's entries are used as a central reference point for all sex-related operations, minimizing discrepancies and simplifying maintenance.
sex_table.rationale: Having a predefined, constant list of sex categories ensures consistent representation and simplifies the process of mapping between internal identifiers and user-facing descriptions, supporting reliable display and comparison logic.

size_table.brief: A constant list of size identifiers representing different size categories.
size_table.details: The `size_table` is a fixed, read-only vector containing size identifiers encapsulated by `size_type` objects, each representing a descriptive size name such as 'tiny', 'small', 'medium', etc. It functions as a central reference for size-related operations, including lookups, formatting, and comparisons across the system. The table is initialized with predefined size categories and is utilized in various parts of the codebase, such as in size lookup functions, display routines, and size-based logic, ensuring consistency in size representation and handling.
size_table.notes: The size identifiers are stored as `size_type` objects within a constant vector, emphasizing their role as immutable reference data used throughout size-related functionalities.
size_table.rationale: This variable exists to provide a standardized set of size categories that facilitate consistent size classification, display, and comparison operations within the system, supporting features like size-based behavior and user-friendly size descriptions.
size_table.name: size_table

field_cand.brief: An array of string labels representing different categories of game entities for flagging purposes.
field_cand.details: The `field_cand` array defines a set of descriptive string labels used to categorize various game entities such as mobiles, players, objects, and rooms. These labels serve as candidates or options when listing, selecting, or managing flags associated with different entity types within the game. The array is referenced in functions like `do_flaglist` to facilitate user interactions by providing clear category options. Each string in the array is initialized with a specific label, ensuring consistent categorization throughout the codebase. The use of the custom `String` class enhances flexibility through features like case-insensitive comparisons, making the flagging system more robust and user-friendly.
field_cand.notes: The array is statically defined and intended for use as a fixed set of category labels. Its design supports straightforward retrieval and display of entity categories during flag management operations.
field_cand.rationale: This variable exists to standardize the categorization of entities that can be flagged, enabling consistent referencing and user interface interactions when managing flags across different entity types.
field_cand.name: field_cand

usages.flag_8cc, void do_flaglist(Character *ch: Accessed to retrieve the string associated with a flag candidate when listing all flag fields.

flag_fields.brief: A constant list defining various flag field categories used for managing game entity attributes and permissions.
flag_fields.details: The `flag_fields` variable is a predefined collection of `field_type` objects, each representing a specific category of flags associated with different game entities such as players, objects, rooms, and characters. Each entry includes a descriptive name, a reference to the relevant flag table, and associated candidate and visibility flags that control how these fields are used, validated, and displayed within the system. This structure enables consistent management of permissions, validation, and user interface interactions across multiple modules. It is initialized with categories that correspond to core entity types and their respective flag tables, ensuring uniform handling of flags throughout the codebase and supporting scalable, modular development.
flag_fields.notes: The `flag_fields` list is designed to centralize flag category definitions, simplifying validation and display logic. It also facilitates dynamic handling of entity attributes, permissions, and visibility settings, which are crucial for maintaining consistent behavior across the system.
flag_fields.rationale: This variable exists to provide a unified, easily maintainable structure for categorizing and managing various game entity attributes through flags. It supports the system's need for organized permission checks, validation, and user interface consistency, enabling flexible and scalable attribute management across different entity types.
flag_fields.name: flag_fields

sector_table.brief: A constant lookup table that associates sector names with their corresponding sector types.
sector_table.details: The `sector_table` is an immutable vector containing pairs of descriptive sector names and their associated `Sector` enumeration values. It provides a centralized reference for translating human-readable sector descriptions into internal identifiers used within the system. This table is initialized at startup and is utilized across various modules for displaying sector information, performing lookups, and supporting environment-related logic. Its structure facilitates efficient iteration and retrieval of sector names based on sector types, ensuring consistency in how sectors are represented and processed throughout the codebase.
sector_table.notes: The table is designed for read-only access, ensuring stability and thread safety. It serves as a key component for user interface displays and sector-specific behaviors.
sector_table.rationale: This variable exists to enable easy mapping between sector descriptions and their internal representations, supporting features such as sector identification, display, and interaction handling within the environment management system.
sector_table.name: sector_table

cont_flags.brief: A constant list of flag definitions representing various states and properties of container objects.
cont_flags.details: The `cont_flags` variable is a constant vector containing `flag_type` structures, each representing a specific property or state of container objects, such as being closable, pickproof, closed, or locked. These flags are used throughout the codebase to check, set, or display container statuses, facilitating consistent handling of container behaviors. The flags are initialized with descriptive names, associated bit constants, and mutability indicators, enabling flexible and clear management of container states in various contexts. This collection supports features like access control, security, and gameplay mechanics related to container interactions.
cont_flags.notes: The flags are stored as a constant vector, ensuring immutability and thread safety. They serve as a reference for interpreting container states and are used in functions that display or modify container properties.
cont_flags.rationale: This variable exists to provide a centralized, standardized set of container property flags, enabling consistent and efficient management of container states across the system, supporting features like locking, security, and user interactions.
cont_flags.name: cont_flags

act_flags.brief: A constant list of flags representing various behaviors, states, and attributes applicable to characters or entities.
act_flags.details: The 'act_flags' variable is a static, constant vector containing multiple 'flag_type' entries, each representing a specific behavior, status, or attribute that can be assigned to characters or entities within the system. These flags include properties such as 'nosummon', 'aggressive', 'pet', 'undead', and class-specific flags like 'cleric' or 'mage'. Each flag is associated with a unique name, a bit identifier, and a mutability indicator, which determines whether the flag can be modified after initialization. The list is used throughout the codebase to check, set, or clear character attributes, influencing behaviors, permissions, and status displays. It is referenced in functions that handle character permissions, behavior checks, and status reporting, ensuring consistent handling of character attributes across the system.
act_flags.notes: Since 'act_flags' is a constant list, it is intended for read-only reference throughout the program. The flags are used in bitwise operations to efficiently manage multiple character attributes simultaneously, facilitating quick checks and modifications.
act_flags.rationale: This variable exists to provide a centralized, standardized collection of character attribute flags, enabling consistent and efficient management of character behaviors and states across various system components.
act_flags.name: act_flags

wear_flags.brief: A constant collection of flags representing various item wear locations on a character.
wear_flags.details: The variable \u2018wear_flags\u2019 is a predefined, immutable vector containing \u2018flag_type\u2019 entries, each corresponding to a specific wear position such as \u2018head\u2019, \u2018legs\u2019, or \u2018wield\u2019. These flags are used throughout the codebase to identify valid locations where items can be equipped or worn by characters. They are initialized at compile time, providing a standardized set of wear options that support item management, searching, and display functionalities. The flags enable consistent handling of item placement logic, ensuring that game mechanics related to equipment are flexible and maintainable.

int flag_index_lookup(const String &name.brief: Finds the index of a flag in a flag table based on a case-insensitive prefix match of its name.
int flag_index_lookup(const String &name.details: The function iterates through each element in the provided vector of flag_type objects, performing a case-insensitive comparison between the first character of the input name and the flag's name. It then checks whether the input name is a prefix of the flag's full name using the is_prefix_of method. If both conditions are satisfied, it returns the current index immediately. If no matching flag is found after examining all entries, it returns -1. This method enables efficient and flexible lookup of flags using partial, case-insensitive names, facilitating user-friendly flag searches and command processing.

params.flag_table: A vector of flag_type objects representing available flags, each with a name and associated properties.
params.name: The name of the flag to search for, as a String object. The search is case-insensitive and based on prefix matching.

int flag_index_lookup(const String &name.returns: The index of the matching flag within the flag_table if found; otherwise, -1.
int flag_index_lookup(const String &name.throws: This function does not throw exceptions; it handles all cases internally.
int flag_index_lookup(const String &name.notes: The function performs a case-insensitive prefix match, which allows for partial and flexible flag searches. It compares only the first character explicitly for case-insensitivity before using the is_prefix_of method for full prefix matching, optimizing performance for common cases.
int flag_index_lookup(const String &name.rationale: This function is used in scenarios where user input or command arguments specify flags by partial or case-insensitive names, such as in flag processing commands or search utilities within the application.
int flag_index_lookup(const String &name.name: flag_index_lookup

usages.flag_8cc, void do_flag(Character *ch, St: Finds the index of a flag in the flag table based on its name during flag processing.

int affect_index_lookup(const String &na.brief: Finds the index of the first affect table entry whose name matches a given prefix case-insensitively.
int affect_index_lookup(const String &na.details: This function performs a linear search through the provided affect_table vector, comparing each entry's name to the input name. It first checks if the initial characters match case-insensitively, then verifies if the input name is a prefix of the current entry's name. If both conditions are satisfied, it returns the index of that entry. If no matching entry is found after examining all entries, it returns -1. This method enables flexible prefix matching and case-insensitive searches, which are useful for partial or user-friendly name lookups within the affect management system.

params.affect_table: A vector of affect_table_type entries to search through.
params.name: The prefix string used to match against affect table entries.

int affect_index_lookup(const String &na.returns: The index of the first matching affect table entry, or -1 if no match is found.
int affect_index_lookup(const String &na.notes: The function assumes that the affect_table vector is not empty and that the name string has at least one character. It performs case-insensitive comparisons for the first character and prefix matching for the entire name.
int affect_index_lookup(const String &na.rationale: This function is used when looking up effects or attributes by name, especially in contexts where partial or case-insensitive matching improves usability, such as parsing user input or configuration data.
int affect_index_lookup(const String &na.name: affect_index_lookup
